title: Images and Media - Bringing Your Pages to Life

seo.title: HTML Images and Media - img, video, audio, figure, alt text, lazy loading | Modern Age Coders
seo.description: Learn HTML images with img tag, video and audio elements, figure and figcaption, alt text for accessibility, and lazy loading. 57 hands-on practice questions.
seo.keywords: html images, img tag, alt text, html video, html audio, figure figcaption, lazy loading, responsive images, source tag, poster attribute

content.what:
What Are Images and Media in HTML?

A web page with only text is boring. Images, videos, and audio are what make web pages come alive. A beautiful photograph, an instructional video, or a background music clip — all of these are added using HTML media tags.

HTML gives you a handful of powerful tags to add media to your pages:

  • <img> — for images
  • <video> — for video files
  • <audio> — for music and sound
  • <figure> and <figcaption> — for images with captions
  • <source> — for providing multiple formats to the browser

All of these are self-closing or specialised tags that let your page show pictures and play media without needing any JavaScript.
content.why:
Why Add Media to Your Pages?

1. Humans Love Visuals

Our brains process images 60,000 times faster than text. A single well-chosen photo can communicate more than a whole paragraph of words. When building a recipe page, a product page, or a blog — images make content instantly understandable and engaging.

2. Accessibility Through Alt Text

Every image should have alt text (alternative text) — a short description that screen readers read aloud for blind users. Alt text also appears if the image fails to load. Good alt text makes your website work for everyone.

3. SEO Benefits

Google cannot 'see' images directly — it reads alt text to understand what an image is about. Pages with descriptive alt text rank higher in image search results. So good alt text means more visitors.

4. Native Video and Audio

Before HTML5 (2014), you needed Flash plugins to play videos. Now, <video> and <audio> are built right into HTML. Any modern browser can play media without installing anything.

5. Creative Freedom

Once you know image and media tags, you can build photo galleries, recipe sites with step images, music players, video tutorials, and so much more. Your pages stop looking like text documents and start looking like real websites.
content.explanation:
Detailed Explanation

The img Tag

The <img> tag is self-closing (no closing tag). It has two essential attributes:

  • src — the source (URL or path to the image file)
  • alt — alternative text describing the image
<img src="cat.jpg" alt="An orange cat sitting on a window sill">

The src can be a relative path (cat.jpg, images/cat.jpg) or a full URL (https://example.com/cat.jpg).

Why alt Text Is Required

The alt attribute is not optional — it is essential. Here is why:

  1. Accessibility: Screen readers announce the alt text to blind users
  2. SEO: Google uses it to understand what your image shows
  3. Fallback: If the image fails to load (slow connection, broken URL), the browser shows the alt text instead

Good alt text describes what the image shows, not just 'image' or 'picture'. For decorative images with no meaning, use alt="" (empty string) — this tells screen readers to skip it.

Width and Height Attributes

You can set the dimensions of an image with width and height attributes (in pixels):

<img src="cat.jpg" alt="A cat" width="300" height="200">

Always set these attributes — they tell the browser how much space to reserve for the image before it loads, which prevents the page layout from jumping around.

figure and figcaption

When an image needs a caption, wrap it in a <figure> element with a <figcaption>:

<figure>
  <img src="eiffel.jpg" alt="Eiffel Tower">
  <figcaption>The Eiffel Tower in Paris, built in 1889.</figcaption>
</figure>

This is the semantic way to associate a caption with an image. Screen readers understand the relationship.

The video Tag

The <video> tag plays video files. Basic syntax:

<video src="movie.mp4" controls width="600">
  Your browser does not support video.
</video>

Common attributes:

  • src — path to the video file
  • controls — shows play/pause/volume controls
  • width and height — video player size
  • autoplay — starts playing automatically (use carefully)
  • muted — starts muted (required for autoplay in most browsers)
  • loop — replays when finished
  • poster — image shown before the video plays
  • preload — how much to preload (none/metadata/auto)

The text inside the <video> tag is shown if the browser cannot play video (very old browsers).

The source Tag for Multiple Formats

Different browsers support different video formats. To be safe, provide multiple formats using <source> tags inside <video>:

<video controls width="600">
  <source src="movie.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  <source src="movie.webm" type="video/webm">
  Your browser does not support video.
</video>

The browser picks the first format it can play. MP4 works almost everywhere, so it should usually be listed first.

The audio Tag

The <audio> tag works similarly to video. It plays sound files:

<audio src="song.mp3" controls>
  Your browser does not support audio.
</audio>

It has the same attributes as video: controls, autoplay, muted, loop, and can also use <source> tags for multiple formats.

Responsive Images with srcset

Modern phones have different screen densities. A 'retina' display needs bigger images than a regular monitor. The srcset attribute lets you provide multiple sizes:

<img src="photo.jpg" srcset="photo-1x.jpg 1x, photo-2x.jpg 2x" alt="Photo">

The browser automatically picks the best size based on the user's screen.

Lazy Loading

By default, browsers load all images on a page immediately. This can slow down pages with many images. The loading="lazy" attribute tells the browser to wait until an image is about to scroll into view:

<img src="big-photo.jpg" alt="A photo" loading="lazy">

This makes pages much faster, especially for image galleries. Use loading="lazy" for images below the fold (not visible when the page first loads).

Where to Get Free Images

For practice and projects, use free image sites that allow commercial use:

  • Unsplash (unsplash.com) — high-quality photos
  • Pexels (pexels.com) — free photos and videos
  • Pixabay (pixabay.com) — photos, illustrations, and videos
  • placeholder.com or via.placeholder.com — placeholder images while developing

Never steal images from random websites — many are copyrighted. Always use images you have permission to use.

codeExamples.title: Basic Image With Alt Text
codeExamples.code:
My Pet

Meet Buddy, my golden retriever.

He loves playing fetch and going for walks.
codeExamples.explanation: A basic image with all the essential attributes: src for the image URL, alt for the description (critical for accessibility and SEO), and width/height to reserve space on the page before the image loads. The placeholder URL shows a blank 400x300 rectangle — replace it with your real image path.
codeExamples.output: A page with a heading 'My Pet', a paragraph, a 400x300 image, and another paragraph.

codeExamples.title: Figure With Caption
codeExamples.code: Famous Indian Landmarks
codeExamples.explanation: The <figure> element groups an image with its caption. The <figcaption> is the caption text — it can be above or below the image. This is the semantic way to add captions to images. Screen readers understand the relationship between the image and caption.
codeExamples.output: A page with two image figures, each with a caption below it describing a famous Indian landmark.

codeExamples.title: Image Gallery
codeExamples.code:
Priya's Photo Gallery

Recent Photos
codeExamples.explanation: A simple gallery with four images, each in its own figure with a caption. All images use loading="lazy" so they only load when scrolled into view — this makes the page load much faster. Every image has descriptive alt text for accessibility.
codeExamples.output: A photo gallery page with four figures, each showing an image and a caption.

codeExamples.title: Video Player With Poster and Controls
codeExamples.code:
Watch My Video

This video shows highlights from my school trip.
codeExamples.explanation: A video player with several best practices: controls shows play/pause/volume buttons. poster is the preview image shown before the video plays. Two <source> tags provide MP4 and WebM formats — the browser picks whichever it supports. The text inside is fallback for very old browsers.
codeExamples.output: A video player with a preview image that shows controls when hovered. Click play to watch the video.

codeExamples.title: Audio Player
codeExamples.code:
Rohan's Favourite Song

Click play to listen:

Title: 'My Melody'
Artist: The Code Band
codeExamples.explanation: A simple audio player. The controls attribute shows the play button, time slider, and volume control. Multiple <source> tags provide MP3 and OGG formats for maximum browser support. MP3 is universal, while OGG is an open format supported by Firefox and Chrome.
codeExamples.output: An audio player with play/pause controls and time slider, ready to play the song.

codeExamples.title: Autoplay Muted Looping Video
codeExamples.code:
Hero Section

This is how modern websites add looping background videos.
codeExamples.explanation: This is how websites add hero-section background videos. autoplay starts the video automatically. muted is required for autoplay in most browsers (they block autoplay with sound). loop replays the video forever. playsinline prevents mobile devices from opening it fullscreen. Use this sparingly — autoplaying videos can be annoying.
codeExamples.output: A video that plays automatically without sound and loops forever, perfect for a website hero section.

commonMistakes.title: Missing alt Attribute
commonMistakes.error: Bad for accessibility and SEO. Screen readers will not describe the image, and the HTML is invalid.
commonMistakes.explanation: Every <img> must have an alt attribute. For decorative images with no meaning, use alt="" (empty string). For meaningful images, describe what they show in a few words.

commonMistakes.title: Wrong Image Path
commonMistakes.error: The image works on Priya's computer but breaks when the website is uploaded to a server.
commonMistakes.explanation: Never use absolute file paths from your computer (like C:\ on Windows). Use relative paths that work on any server. Put your images in a folder inside your project, like images/ or assets/, and link using images/cat.jpg.

commonMistakes.title: Forgetting the controls Attribute on Video
commonMistakes.error: The video shows but there are no play/pause buttons — users cannot control it.
commonMistakes.explanation: Without the controls attribute, the video has no visible interface. Add controls so users can play, pause, and adjust volume. For background videos, omit controls but add autoplay muted loop.

commonMistakes.title: Autoplay Video Without muted
commonMistakes.error: Modern browsers block autoplay videos that have sound — the video does not play at all.
commonMistakes.explanation: Browsers now require muted for autoplay to work, to stop annoying websites from blasting sound. If you want autoplay, add muted as well. Users can then manually unmute if they want.

commonMistakes.title: Using Full Computer Path Instead of URL
commonMistakes.error: The image does not load for other users because they do not have that file.
commonMistakes.explanation: Never use file:/// URLs — they only work on your own computer. Either upload the image to a web server and use its URL, or keep it in your project folder and use a relative path.

summary: The tag displays images. It is self-closing and requires both src (image source) and alt (description).
Alt text is essential — it describes the image for screen readers, appears when the image fails to load, and helps SEO.
Always set width and height attributes on images to prevent the page layout from jumping around while images load.
Wrap images with captions in <figure> and use <figcaption> for the caption text. This is the semantic way to add captions.
The <video> tag plays video files. Use the controls attribute to show play/pause buttons and poster to set a preview image.
The <audio> tag plays sound files. It has the same controls, autoplay, muted, and loop attributes as video.
Use <source> tags inside <video> and <audio> to provide multiple formats — the browser picks the first one it supports.
For autoplay to work in modern browsers, you must also add the muted attribute. Otherwise browsers block autoplay.
Use loading="lazy" on images below the fold to make your page load faster — images load only when about to scroll into view.
Get free images from Unsplash, Pexels, or Pixabay. Never steal copyrighted images from random websites.
Use relative paths like 'images/cat.jpg' for files in your project, or full URLs starting with https:// for external images.

topicSpecific.question:
What does this HTML display?
<img src="cat.jpg" alt="A cat">
topicSpecific.hint: It is an image tag.
topicSpecific.answer: An image from 'cat.jpg' with the alt text 'A cat' shown if the image fails to load.
topicSpecific.explanation: The <img> tag displays an image. The src attribute is the image source and alt is the alternative text for accessibility.

topicSpecific.question: Write HTML for an image called 'sunset.jpg' with alt text 'A beautiful orange sunset at the beach'.
topicSpecific.hint: Use with src and alt.
topicSpecific.answer: <img src="sunset.jpg" alt="A beautiful orange sunset at the beach">
topicSpecific.explanation: Every image needs src for the file and alt for the description. Good alt text describes what the image shows.

topicSpecific.question:
This image has an accessibility problem. Fix it:
<img src="dog.jpg">
topicSpecific.hint: Screen readers need help.
topicSpecific.answer: <img src="dog.jpg" alt="A brown dog playing in the park">
topicSpecific.explanation: The alt attribute is missing. Screen readers cannot describe the image to blind users without alt text. Always include a meaningful description.

topicSpecific.question: What does the alt attribute do?
topicSpecific.hint: It has multiple purposes.
topicSpecific.answer: It provides alternative text that is shown if the image fails to load, read by screen readers for accessibility, and used by search engines.
topicSpecific.explanation: Alt text serves three purposes: accessibility (screen readers), fallback (if image fails), and SEO (search engines understand the image content).

topicSpecific.question: Write HTML for an image that is 500 pixels wide and 300 pixels tall.
topicSpecific.hint: Use width and height attributes.
topicSpecific.answer: <img src="photo.jpg" alt="A photo" width="500" height="300">
topicSpecific.explanation: Setting width and height reserves space on the page for the image before it loads, preventing layout shifts.

topicSpecific.question:
What is wrong with this img tag?
<img scr="cat.jpg" alt="Cat">
topicSpecific.hint: Check the attribute name carefully.
topicSpecific.answer: <img src="cat.jpg" alt="Cat">
topicSpecific.explanation: The attribute is src, not scr. This is a very common typo. The image will not load with the wrong attribute name.

topicSpecific.hint: figure groups an image with its caption.
topicSpecific.answer: An image of a tiger with a caption 'A Bengal tiger in the jungle' shown below (or sometimes above) it.
topicSpecific.explanation: The <figure> element groups an image and its <figcaption>. The caption is semantically associated with the image.

topicSpecific.question: Write HTML for a video with controls and a width of 500 pixels.
topicSpecific.hint: Use <video> with src, controls, and width.
topicSpecific.answer:
<video src="movie.mp4" controls width="500">
  Your browser does not support video.
</video>
topicSpecific.explanation: The controls attribute shows play/pause buttons. width sets the player width in pixels. The text inside is fallback for old browsers.

topicSpecific.question: What does the controls attribute do on a video tag?
topicSpecific.hint: It is about the user interface.
topicSpecific.answer: It shows the built-in video player controls: play/pause button, time slider, volume control, and fullscreen button.
topicSpecific.explanation: Without controls, the video has no visible buttons. Users cannot play, pause, or control it (unless you add custom JavaScript controls).

topicSpecific.question:
Fix this audio tag so users can play it:
<audio src="song.mp3"></audio>
topicSpecific.hint: Something is needed for the user to interact.
topicSpecific.answer: <audio src="song.mp3" controls></audio>
topicSpecific.explanation: Without controls, the audio element has no visible interface. Users cannot play it. Add controls to show the built-in player.

topicSpecific.question: Write an HTML image tag that uses lazy loading.
topicSpecific.hint: Use loading="lazy".
topicSpecific.answer: <img src="big-photo.jpg" alt="A big photo" loading="lazy">
topicSpecific.explanation: The loading="lazy" attribute tells the browser to delay loading the image until it is about to scroll into view. This makes pages faster.

topicSpecific.question: What does the poster attribute do on a video?
topicSpecific.hint: It is about what users see before clicking play.
topicSpecific.answer: It sets the image shown before the video plays. Like a thumbnail or preview image.
topicSpecific.explanation: poster="image.jpg" displays that image where the video will play until the user clicks play. It is like a movie poster for your video.

topicSpecific.question:
This autoplay video does not play. Fix it:
<video src="video.mp4" autoplay controls></video>
topicSpecific.hint: Modern browsers need one more attribute for autoplay.
topicSpecific.answer: <video src="video.mp4" autoplay muted controls></video>
topicSpecific.explanation: Modern browsers block autoplay videos with sound. Add muted along with autoplay so the browser allows it to play automatically.

topicSpecific.question: Add a video with a poster image 'thumbnail.jpg' and controls, with the video source 'tutorial.mp4'.
topicSpecific.hint: Combine video, source, controls, and poster.
topicSpecific.answer:
<video controls poster="thumbnail.jpg" width="600">
  <source src="tutorial.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  Your browser does not support video.
</video>
topicSpecific.explanation: The poster attribute shows 'thumbnail.jpg' before the video plays. controls gives the user play/pause buttons. The source tag specifies the video file and its type.

topicSpecific.question: Why use multiple <source> tags inside a video?
topicSpecific.hint: Different browsers support different formats.
topicSpecific.answer: Different browsers support different video formats. Providing multiple sources (like MP4 and WebM) ensures the browser can find one it supports.
topicSpecific.explanation: Browsers try each <source> in order and use the first format they support. MP4 is almost universal, but WebM is sometimes preferred. Providing both is a safety net.

topicSpecific.question:
Why will this image not load?
<img src="C:\Users\Rohan\photo.jpg" alt="Photo">
topicSpecific.hint: The path is specific to Rohan's computer.
topicSpecific.explanation: Absolute file paths from your computer only work on that computer. When you upload the website, the path breaks. Always use relative paths or web URLs.

topicSpecific.question: Write HTML for a gallery of 4 images with captions using figure and figcaption.
topicSpecific.hint: Four figure elements, each with an img and figcaption.
topicSpecific.answer:
<figure>
  <img src="photo1.jpg" alt="Photo 1">
  <figcaption>Caption for photo 1</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure>
  <img src="photo2.jpg" alt="Photo 2">
  <figcaption>Caption for photo 2</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure>
  <img src="photo3.jpg" alt="Photo 3">
  <figcaption>Caption for photo 3</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure>
  <img src="photo4.jpg" alt="Photo 4">
  <figcaption>Caption for photo 4</figcaption>
</figure>
topicSpecific.explanation: Each image gets its own figure with a figcaption. This is the semantic way to build image galleries where each image has a caption.

topicSpecific.question: What does loading="lazy" do?
topicSpecific.hint: It is about performance.
topicSpecific.answer: It delays loading the image until the user is about to scroll it into view, making the initial page load faster.
topicSpecific.explanation: Lazy loading improves performance. Instead of loading all images at once, the browser only loads each image when it is needed. This is especially useful for long pages with many images.

topicSpecific.question:
This video has no sound and users want to hear it. What is wrong?
<video src="song.mp4" autoplay muted loop controls></video>
topicSpecific.hint: Look at all the attributes.
topicSpecific.answer: Remove muted if you want sound. But then autoplay may not work. Either remove muted and accept that the user must click play, or keep muted for autoplay and let users unmute.
topicSpecific.explanation: The muted attribute forces the video to start without sound. If you want sound, remove it — but know that browsers will then block autoplay. It is a tradeoff.

topicSpecific.question: Write an audio player with two source formats: 'song.mp3' and 'song.ogg', with controls.
topicSpecific.hint: audio tag with two source tags.
topicSpecific.answer:
<audio controls>
  <source src="song.mp3" type="audio/mpeg">
  <source src="song.ogg" type="audio/ogg">
  Your browser does not support audio.
</audio>
topicSpecific.explanation: Multiple audio formats ensure compatibility. MP3 works everywhere, OGG is supported by Firefox and Chrome. The browser picks the first one it can play.

topicSpecific.question: What does the empty alt attribute mean: alt=""?
topicSpecific.hint: It is not a mistake.
topicSpecific.answer: An empty alt (alt="") tells screen readers that the image is decorative and should be skipped. It is valid and sometimes correct.
topicSpecific.explanation: For purely decorative images (like dividers, background patterns), use alt="". This tells screen readers the image has no meaningful content. Never just omit alt — always include it, even if empty.

topicSpecific.question:
This image is 4000 pixels wide but the page shows it as 500px. Fix it properly:
<img src="huge-photo.jpg" alt="Photo" style="width:500px">
topicSpecific.hint: Using CSS to shrink a huge image wastes bandwidth.
topicSpecific.answer:
Resize the actual image file to 500px first (using an image editor), then: 
<img src="photo-500.jpg" alt="Photo" width="500" height="333">
topicSpecific.explanation: Using CSS to shrink a huge image still downloads the full 4000px file, wasting bandwidth and slowing the page. Resize the source image file first, then use proper width/height attributes.

topicSpecific.question: Add a video with a poster and controls that uses lazy loading — use attributes: width 600, controls, poster 'thumb.jpg', preload metadata.
topicSpecific.hint: Video does not have loading="lazy" directly, but has preload attribute.
topicSpecific.answer:
<video width="600" controls poster="thumb.jpg" preload="metadata">
  <source src="video.mp4" type="video/mp4">
</video>
topicSpecific.explanation: For videos, use preload="metadata" to load only the video's duration and dimensions initially, not the whole file. The poster shows as a preview. This achieves similar savings to lazy loading.

topicSpecific.question:
Ananya wants her image to be centered on the page but it is aligned left. Fix the HTML (not using CSS):
<img src="photo.jpg" alt="Photo" width="400">
topicSpecific.hint: HTML alone cannot center easily, but you can wrap in a block element.
topicSpecific.answer:
<figure style="text-align:center">
  <img src="photo.jpg" alt="Photo" width="400">
</figure>
<!-- Or better: use CSS externally -->
topicSpecific.explanation: HTML alone does not have a 'center' attribute anymore (it was removed). You need CSS to center images. The cleanest way is to use CSS in a stylesheet, but inline style on a wrapper element works too.

topicSpecific.question: Write a complete HTML page with a heading, an intro paragraph, a figure with an image and caption, a video player with poster, and an audio player. Use proper semantics and lazy loading where appropriate.
topicSpecific.hint: Combine figure, video, audio all in a complete page.
topicSpecific.answer:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <title>Media Showcase</title>
</head>
<body>
  <h1>My Media Showcase</h1>
  <p>This page demonstrates HTML images, video, and audio.</p>

  <figure>
    <img src="photo.jpg" alt="My photo" width="400" loading="lazy">
    <figcaption>A photo from my recent trip</figcaption>
  </figure>

  <h2>Watch My Video</h2>
  <video width="600" controls poster="thumb.jpg">
    <source src="video.mp4" type="video/mp4">
    Your browser does not support video.
  </video>

  <h2>Listen to My Song</h2>
  <audio controls>
    <source src="song.mp3" type="audio/mpeg">
    Your browser does not support audio.
  </audio>
</body>
</html>
topicSpecific.explanation: This page showcases all three media tags: figure with image, video with poster, and audio player. The image uses lazy loading and proper alt text. The video has a preview poster and controls. The audio has fallback text for old browsers.

mixed.question: What attribute is required on every img tag?
mixed.explanation: Every image must have alt text — either describing the image or empty (alt="") for decorative images.

mixed.question: Write HTML for an image 'logo.png' with alt 'Company Logo', width 200, height 100.
mixed.explanation: Setting width and height helps the browser reserve space for the image before it loads, preventing layout jumps.

mixed.question:
What is wrong?
<img></img>
mixed.hint: img has specific rules about attributes and closing.
mixed.answer: The img tag is missing src and alt, and also has an unnecessary closing tag. Correct: <img src="pic.jpg" alt="Description">
mixed.explanation: The img tag is self-closing — no </img> needed. It also requires src (source) and alt (description).

mixed.question: What tag is used for sound files?
mixed.explanation: <audio> plays sound files like MP3, WAV, and OGG. It works similarly to the video tag.

mixed.question: Write HTML for a figure with an image 'beach.jpg' (alt 'Sandy beach') and caption 'Visit Goa'.
mixed.explanation: figure groups an image with its caption. figcaption provides the caption text below (or above) the image.

mixed.question: What is the difference between and ?
mixed.hint: Both show the image but behave differently for some users.
mixed.answer: Both show the image, but only the second has alt text, which is essential for screen readers, search engines, and fallback display if the image fails to load.
mixed.explanation: Without alt, the image is invisible to blind users and search engines cannot understand it. Always include alt text.

mixed.question: Write HTML for a video that autoplays, loops, and has no sound (background video).
mixed.explanation: For a background-style video: autoplay starts it, muted is required for autoplay to work, and loop makes it replay forever. No controls needed for decorative background.

mixed.question:
Vikram's image loads with a long delay. What attribute can make it faster for below-the-fold images?
<img src="big-photo.jpg" alt="Photo">
mixed.explanation: loading="lazy" delays loading until the image scrolls into view. Use this for images below the fold (not visible on initial page load).

mixed.question:
What does this code produce?
<audio src="song.mp3" controls autoplay></audio>
mixed.answer: An audio player with controls. However, in most modern browsers, autoplay with sound is blocked unless the user has interacted with the page first.
mixed.explanation: Browsers block autoplay audio with sound to prevent annoying users. The audio will usually need a user click before it plays. Adding muted is not meaningful for audio since audio is all about sound.

mixed.question: Write HTML for a video tag with two source formats: MP4 ('clip.mp4') and WebM ('clip.webm'), with controls.
mixed.answer:
<video controls width="600">
  <source src="clip.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  <source src="clip.webm" type="video/webm">
  Your browser does not support video.
</video>
mixed.explanation: Multiple source tags let the browser pick the format it supports. MP4 works everywhere and should usually be listed first.

mixed.question:
Why might this image take forever to load?
<img src="https://example.com/huge-8000x6000.jpg" alt="Photo" width="400">
mixed.answer: The image is downloaded at full 8000x6000 resolution even though only 400px is displayed. Use a properly sized image file instead.
mixed.explanation: Setting width in HTML only changes the display size, not the download size. You still download the full file. For fast pages, save the image at the size you will actually display.

mixed.question:
What does this do?
<img src="photo.jpg" alt="" loading="lazy">
mixed.answer: Loads a decorative image (marked as decorative with empty alt) lazily — only when it scrolls into view. Screen readers skip it.
mixed.explanation: Empty alt means the image is decorative and has no semantic meaning. Screen readers skip it. Lazy loading delays loading until needed.

mixed.question: Write HTML for a complete photo gallery page with 3 figures, each containing an image (placeholder URLs), alt text, figcaption, and lazy loading. Include full boilerplate.
mixed.answer:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <title>Gallery</title>
</head>
<body>
  <h1>My Gallery</h1>
  <figure>
    <img src="https://via.placeholder.com/400x300" alt="Mountain landscape" width="400" height="300" loading="lazy">
    <figcaption>Himalayas at sunrise</figcaption>
  </figure>
  <figure>
    <img src="https://via.placeholder.com/400x300" alt="Beach with waves" width="400" height="300" loading="lazy">
    <figcaption>Goa beach</figcaption>
  </figure>
  <figure>
    <img src="https://via.placeholder.com/400x300" alt="City skyline" width="400" height="300" loading="lazy">
    <figcaption>Mumbai skyline</figcaption>
  </figure>
</body>
</html>
mixed.explanation: A complete gallery page with three figures. Each image has descriptive alt text, proper dimensions, lazy loading, and a caption. This is a production-quality gallery structure.
Three issues: (1) missing alt attributes hurt accessibility and SEO, (2) huge file sizes slow download, (3) no lazy loading means all images download at once.

mixed.question: What does preload="none" do on a video?
mixed.hint: It is about what the browser downloads.
mixed.answer: It tells the browser not to preload any video data — only download when the user clicks play. Saves bandwidth but adds a delay when user plays.
mixed.explanation: The preload attribute has three values: none (download nothing), metadata (only info like duration), and auto (download whole video). Use none or metadata for performance.

mcqs.question: Which tag displays an image?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The <img> tag displays images. <image> is not a valid tag. <picture> exists but wraps multiple sources.

mcqs.question: Which attribute specifies the image file?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. src (source) points to the image file. href is for links, not images.

mcqs.question: Why is the alt attribute important?

options.C: It is for accessibility, SEO, and fallback if the image fails to load
options.D: It is optional and never needed

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. alt text serves three purposes: screen readers use it, search engines use it, and it displays if the image fails to load.

mcqs.question: Which tag plays video files?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The <video> tag plays video files.

mcqs.question: Which attribute shows play/pause buttons on a video?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. The controls attribute shows the built-in video player controls.

mcqs.question: Which element wraps an image with its caption?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. <figure> wraps an image and its <figcaption> caption.

mcqs.question: Which tag plays audio files?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. The <audio> tag plays audio files like MP3 and WAV.

mcqs.question: What does loading="lazy" do?

options.A: Loads the image slowly on purpose
options.B: Delays loading until the image is about to be visible

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Lazy loading delays loading until the image scrolls into view, making pages load faster initially.

mcqs.question: What does the poster attribute do?

options.B: Sets the image shown before the video plays
options.D: Makes the video into a poster

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. poster is the preview image shown before the video starts playing.

mcqs.question: Why provide multiple <source> tags in a video?

options.A: To play multiple videos at once
options.B: To support different browsers with different format preferences

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Different browsers support different video formats. Multiple sources let the browser pick the first format it supports.

mcqs.question: To autoplay a video in a modern browser, which attribute must be included?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. Modern browsers block autoplay videos with sound. You must also include muted for autoplay to work.

mcqs.question: Which is a valid image alt attribute for a decorative divider line?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. For purely decorative images, use empty alt (alt=""). This tells screen readers to skip it. Never omit alt entirely.

mcqs.question: Which is a good source of free images?

options.D: Stock photo sites that require payment

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Unsplash, Pexels, and Pixabay offer free, legal images you can use in your projects. Never steal copyrighted images.

mcqs.question: Which tag element wraps the image caption?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. <figcaption> is the caption for an image inside a <figure>.

mcqs.question: What happens if an image with no alt attribute fails to load?

options.C: Shows a broken image icon (or nothing meaningful)

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. Without alt text, failed images just show a broken icon. With alt, the alt text displays instead, giving context.

mcqs.question: Why should you set width and height attributes on images?

options.B: To reserve space and prevent layout shift while loading

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. When the browser knows the dimensions, it can reserve the right amount of space before the image loads, preventing content from jumping around.

mcqs.question: What is the best way to handle a video that needs to autoplay as a background?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. Background videos need autoplay + muted (so browser allows autoplay) + loop (so it replays). No controls since it is decorative.

mcqs.question: Which is TRUE about responsive images?

options.B: You can use srcset to provide multiple sizes for different screen densities
options.C: You always load the largest version

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The srcset attribute lets you provide multiple image sizes. The browser picks the best one for the user's screen.

mcqs.question: Which is valid HTML for the img tag?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. img is self-closing, needs src and alt, and uses <img> not <image>.

mcqs.question: Why should you NOT use absolute file paths like C:/photos/cat.jpg in img src?

options.B: They only work on your computer and break when the site is uploaded

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Absolute paths from your computer only work locally. When you upload the site to a server, those paths no longer exist. Always use relative paths or URLs.

codingChallenges.title: Simple Image With Alt
codingChallenges.question: Create a complete HTML page with a heading 'My Pet' and an image of a pet with proper alt text and dimensions. Use a placeholder URL from via.placeholder.com.
codingChallenges.expectedOutput: A page with a heading and an image that has all the essential attributes.
codingChallenges.constraints: Must include alt, width, and height attributes.
codingChallenges.solution:
My Pet Dog

Meet Buddy, my golden retriever who loves playing fetch.
codingChallenges.explanation: A basic image with all essential attributes: src for the source, alt for accessibility and SEO, width and height for layout stability.
codingChallenges.alternativeSolution:
My Cat

This is my cat Mittens.

codingChallenges.title: Image With Caption
codingChallenges.question: Create a complete HTML page with a figure containing an image and a figcaption. Topic: a famous Indian landmark.
codingChallenges.expectedOutput: A page with a figure containing an image and a caption describing the landmark.
codingChallenges.constraints: Must use figure and figcaption elements.
codingChallenges.solution: Famous Indian Landmarks
codingChallenges.explanation: The figure element groups an image with its caption. figcaption provides semantic meaning — this is the description of the image above it.

codingChallenges.title: Image Gallery of 4 Photos
codingChallenges.question: Build a complete HTML page titled 'My Photo Gallery' with 4 figures, each containing an image, alt text, and figcaption. Use lazy loading on all images. Include a heading.
codingChallenges.expectedOutput: A photo gallery page with 4 captioned images, all lazy-loaded.
codingChallenges.constraints: All 4 images must use figure, figcaption, descriptive alt, and loading="lazy".
codingChallenges.solution: My Photo Gallery
codingChallenges.explanation: A complete gallery with 4 captioned figures. Lazy loading improves performance — images load only when scrolled into view. Each alt text describes what the image shows.
codingChallenges.alternativeSolution: Gallery

codingChallenges.title: Video Player With Poster
codingChallenges.question: Create a complete HTML page with a video player. The video should have controls, a poster image, width 600, and use a source tag to specify an MP4 file.
codingChallenges.expectedOutput: A page with a video player showing a poster image, controls, and 600px width.
codingChallenges.constraints: Must use video tag with controls, poster, width, and source tag.
codingChallenges.solution:
Watch My Video

Click play to watch the video.
codingChallenges.explanation: The video player has all the best-practice attributes: controls for user interaction, poster for the preview image, width for size, and a source tag specifying the MP4 file. Fallback text appears only for very old browsers.
codingChallenges.alternativeSolution: Video

codingChallenges.title: Audio Player
codingChallenges.question: Create a complete HTML page with an audio player for a song. Include controls, provide two source formats (MP3 and OGG), and add a heading and description.
codingChallenges.expectedOutput: A page with an audio player that has controls and multiple source formats.
codingChallenges.constraints: Must use audio tag with controls and two source tags.
codingChallenges.solution:
Listen to My Song

A peaceful melody for relaxation.

Song: Peaceful Morning
Artist: Aarav Singh
codingChallenges.explanation: An audio player with multiple source formats ensures maximum compatibility. MP3 works everywhere, OGG is an open format supported by Firefox and Chrome.
codingChallenges.alternativeSolution: My Music

codingChallenges.title: Recipe Page With Images
codingChallenges.question: Build a complete recipe page for 'Chocolate Chip Cookies' with a heading, description, a main image (figure with caption), a list of ingredients, and three step images (each in a figure with figcaption). Use lazy loading.
codingChallenges.expectedOutput: A complete recipe page with multiple captioned images and lazy loading.
codingChallenges.constraints: Must use figure and figcaption for all images, lazy loading, descriptive alt text, and proper HTML structure.
codingChallenges.solution:
Chocolate Chip Cookies

Learn how to make soft and chewy chocolate chip cookies at home.

Ingredients

  • 2 cups flour
  • 1 cup butter
  • 1 cup sugar
  • 2 eggs
  • 1 cup chocolate chips

Steps
codingChallenges.explanation: A complete recipe page with a main hero image (not lazy loaded since it is above the fold), an ingredients list, and three step-by-step images (all lazy loaded because they are below the fold). Every image has descriptive alt text and a caption.
codingChallenges.alternativeSolution:
Chocolate Chip Cookies

Ingredients

  • Flour, butter, sugar, eggs, chocolate chips

Steps

codingChallenges.title: Multi-Media Page
codingChallenges.question: Create a complete page that demonstrates all three media types — image (in figure with caption and lazy loading), video (with controls, poster, and two source formats), and audio (with controls and two source formats). Include a heading for each section.
codingChallenges.expectedOutput: A full page showcasing image, video, and audio tags with best practices.
codingChallenges.constraints: Must include all three media types with best practice attributes.
codingChallenges.solution:
My Media Showcase

This page demonstrates images, video, and audio in HTML.

Featured Image

Watch the Video

Listen to the Audio
codingChallenges.explanation: A comprehensive media showcase demonstrating every best practice: figure/figcaption for images, multiple video sources for compatibility, multiple audio sources, and proper alt text and semantic structure.
codingChallenges.alternativeSolution:
Media Types

Image

Video

Audio

codingChallenges.expectedOutput: A full portfolio home page with navigation, profile image, video, image gallery, and contact info.
codingChallenges.constraints: Must include nav, figure for images with captions, video with controls and poster, lazy loading, and contact links.
codingChallenges.solution:
Home | Projects | Contact

Ananya Singh
